race.name: El Tour de Tucson
race.tagline: 102 miles through the Sonoran Desert. Saguaro cacti, four mountain ranges, and the best November cycling weather in America. The season closer.

terrain_types: Flat to gently rolling desert roads
vitals.start_time: Saturday 7:00 AM (102-mile Century); 9:00 AM (63-mile Metric); 10:00 AM (32-mile Half Metric)
vitals.registration: Online via eltourdetucson.org. Early-bird pricing available. $85-130 depending on distance and registration date. Platinum (competitive 102-mile) and standard categories.
vitals.prize_purse: None. Charity fundraising event — participants raise funds for nonprofits. $130M+ raised since 1983.
vitals.aid_stations: 8-10 aid stations on the 102-mile route, approximately every 7-12 miles. Staffed with water, bananas, oranges, cookies, pretzels, port-o-pots, and medical support.
vitals.cutoff_time: All routes close at 4:30 PM. Aid stations close on a rolling schedule to accommodate the deadline.
vitals.entry_fee: $85-130 (varies by distance and registration date; Platinum 102-mile at higher tier)

climate.description: November in Tucson is arguably the best cycling weather in America. Daytime temperatures of 49-71°F (9-22°C) with low humidity, clear skies, and abundant sunshine. The Sonoran Desert in autumn is comfortable and dry. This is the event's greatest asset — a warm, dry escape when most of the country has packed the bikes away for winter.
challenges: Morning starts can be cool (45-55°F) — arm warmers and a vest useful for the first hour
Sun exposure despite moderate temperatures — desert UV at 2,400 feet is intense
Dry air requires proactive hydration despite comfortable temperatures
Occasional wind on exposed desert sections, especially the southern return leg

terrain.primary: Flat to gently rolling Sonoran Desert
terrain.surface: Paved city and county roads circling greater Tucson. The terrain is predominantly flat to gently rolling with 3,007 feet of climbing across 102 miles. The course is a perimeter loop starting and finishing at the Tucson Convention Center, passing through Sonoran Desert landscape with saguaro cacti, mesquite, palo verde, and views of four surrounding mountain ranges.
features: Flat to gently rolling — 3,007 ft of climbing across 102 miles
Sonoran Desert scenery — saguaro cacti, desert flora, mountain backdrop
Four mountain ranges visible: Santa Catalina, Rincon, Tucson, Santa Rita
Paved roads throughout — no technical demands
Passes through/near Saguaro National Park, Old Spanish Trail, Davis-Monthan AFB, Colossal Cave area

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: America's premier November century ride and one of the largest perimeter cycling events in the country. The Sonoran Desert loop is flat and scenic, but the real draw is the timing — perfect cycling conditions when most of the US is done for the season. T3 by score (57). Cultural impact bumped to 3 for $130M+ in charity fundraising over 42 years.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Climate=2 (near-ideal weather is a benefit, not a challenge). Community=4 (massive participatory event with strong charity focus). Expenses=4 (Tucson is affordable). Elevation=1 and technicality=1 reflect a flat, non-technical paved course. Cultural_impact=3 for exceptional charitable legacy and regional significance.

logistics.explanation: Tucson International Airport (TUS) has direct flights from most major US cities. Phoenix Sky Harbor (PHX) is a 90-minute drive with significantly more flight options. The city is compact and navigable. 42+ years of event management mean the logistics are dialed — road closures, aid stations, staggered start corrals, and parking all run smoothly. Packet pickup at the expo is straightforward. Start/finish at Tucson Convention Center puts everything within walking distance of downtown hotels.

length.explanation: 102 miles is a proper century. The flat terrain means the competitive front finishes in under 4 hours and average riders in 5.5-7. The distance is the challenge, not the terrain. Five distance options (102, 63, 32 miles plus fun rides) make this accessible from first-timers to veterans. The 4:30 PM cutoff is generous for the century but enforced — aid stations close on schedule.

technicality.explanation: Paved roads, flat terrain, no technical demands whatsoever. This is a fitness and endurance test, not a bike-handling test. The most technical challenge is navigating the crowded first miles in the mass start with 9,000+ riders funneling onto city streets.

elevation.explanation: 3,007 feet of climbing across 102 miles — roughly 30 feet per mile. Tucson sits at about 2,400 feet in a basin ringed by mountains, but the course stays in the basin. Gentle rollers through the southern and eastern sections, nothing that qualifies as a real climb. The route touches 3,800 feet near Colossal Cave on Old Spanish Trail before descending back.

climate.explanation: November in Tucson is near-perfect cycling weather. 49-71°F, dry, sunny, with temperatures rarely below 39° or above 82°. This is the opposite of a climate challenge — the weather is the event's strongest selling point. Morning starts are cool, midday is warm and pleasant, and rain probability is near zero. The climate score is low because the weather helps you, not hurts you. Only deductions for dry-air dehydration risk and intense desert UV.

altitude.explanation: Tucson sits at about 2,400 feet with the course ranging from 2,400 to 3,800 feet. Negligible altitude effect for virtually all riders. No acclimatization needed.

adventure.explanation: The Sonoran Desert is genuinely beautiful in its own austere way — towering saguaro cacti standing sentinel, four mountain ranges creating a dramatic 360-degree backdrop, the desert light at sunrise through the first hour. The route passes near Saguaro National Park and historic Colossal Cave. It's not mountain or coastal scenery, but the landscape has real character. The adventure is geographic novelty — riding through a desert ecosystem most cyclists never experience.

prestige.explanation: One of America's oldest and largest century rides, running since 1983 with 42+ editions. Branded as 'America's Biggest Bike Race.' The event has attracted professional cyclists including Damiano Cunego (Giro d'Italia champion), Chloe Dygert (Olympic gold medalist), Quinn Simmons, Travis McCabe, and Frankie Andreu. 'I did El Tour' is recognized in US cycling circles. But it's fundamentally a participatory charity ride, not a competitive race — the prestige is in the community and tradition, not the competitive result.

race_quality.explanation: 42+ years of organizational experience show in the execution. Aid stations are well-stocked with desert-appropriate provisions every 7-12 miles. Medical support is solid. Road closures and police support are handled professionally. Start waves are well-managed with Platinum riders getting priority positioning. But it's a mass-participation event for 9,000+ riders, not a boutique race experience — expect crowded starts, variable pacing groups, and the organizational compromises inherent to events this size.

experience.explanation: Riding 102 miles through the Sonoran Desert in November sunshine, with saguaros and mountain ranges as your backdrop, is a genuinely pleasant experience. The route through Old Spanish Trail and the eastern desert sections is the scenic highlight. Post-ride atmosphere at Tucson Convention Center is warm and celebratory. But the course is flat and suburban-adjacent — significant portions pass through residential and commercial Tucson. The emotional peak is the November timing: one last great outdoor ride before winter.

community.explanation: 9,000-11,000 riders make this one of the largest cycling events in the US. The charity component is the event's soul — $130M+ raised for nonprofits since 1983. The field draws a genuinely broad cross-section: competitive platinum cyclists, charity team riders, first-time century completers, snowbird retirees, local cycling clubs, and families on the shorter routes. Tucson's cycling community is passionate and welcoming. The event functions as a season-closing celebration that unites the cycling calendar.

field_depth.explanation: Primarily participatory, though the Platinum category on the 102-mile course attracts legitimate competitors. Tyler Stites set a 3:30:33 course record in 2024; Chloe Dygert broke the women's record the same year. The front group includes strong domestic riders and occasional visiting pros. But this is not a competitive race by design — the depth is in participation breadth, not racing depth. If you want to race, there are better options.

value.explanation: $85-130 is mid-range for a US century ride. You get a well-organized event with 42+ years of experience, 8-10 aid stations, a full expo weekend, a finisher medal, and the infrastructure that comes with managing 9,000+ riders. Fair value but not exceptional — the entry fee has crept up over the years. The real value proposition is the November timing and Tucson's cycling-friendly destination appeal.

expenses.explanation: Tucson is affordable for a US cycling destination. Hotels near the Convention Center run $115-230/night; airport-area hotels from $99. Food is excellent and cheap — Tucson is a UNESCO City of Gastronomy with outstanding Mexican and Southwestern cuisine at very reasonable prices. Flights to TUS are affordable from most US cities. Budget $500-800 for a long weekend including travel, lodging, entry, and food. This is one of the cheaper cycling destination weekends in America.

race.biased_opinion:
El Tour de Tucson is the ride you do in November when everyone else has put their bikes away. The course is flat, the terrain is non-technical, and the climbing is negligible — 3,007 feet across 102 miles barely registers. None of that matters. What matters is riding 100+ miles through the Sonoran Desert in perfect 65-degree sunshine, with saguaro cacti and four mountain ranges as your backdrop, while your friends back east are staring at their trainers. The event's genius is timing: it's a season-ending celebration disguised as a century ride, and 9,000+ riders show up every year because cycling in November sunshine is a gift that Tucson delivers reliably.

The other thing El Tour gets right is accessibility. This is not a suffer-fest designed to break you. It's a community ride that welcomes first-time century riders alongside Platinum-category racers who finish in under 4 hours. The charity backbone — $130 million raised since 1983 — gives the whole weekend a sense of purpose beyond personal achievement. You're riding for something bigger, through a landscape most cyclists never see, in weather most cyclists can only dream about in late November. The competitive front end has attracted names like Chloe Dygert and Damiano Cunego, but the heart of El Tour is the 7,000 riders behind them who just want one more great day on the bike before winter.

final_verdict.one_liner: 102 miles through the Sonoran Desert in perfect November weather. America's season closer.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want one last great outdoor ride before winter. Yes if you want a well-organized century in beautiful weather with a massive community and charitable purpose. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or serious competitive racing — this is a participatory celebration, not a suffer-fest.
alternatives: Hotter'N Hell Hundred — if you want a hot-weather century with more suffering (August in Wichita Falls, Texas)
Tour de Palm Springs — if you want a similar desert century with a charity focus
Cape Town Cycle Tour — if you want a massive international road ride with comparable scenery and community

logistics.nearest_airport: Tucson International Airport (TUS) — 8 miles south of downtown, direct flights from most major US cities including Denver, Dallas, Chicago, LA, Phoenix. Phoenix Sky Harbor (PHX) is 90 minutes north with significantly more flight options and often cheaper fares.
logistics.travel_tips: Tucson is compact and easy to navigate. The start/finish at Tucson Convention Center is in downtown, accessible via SunLink streetcar and SunTran bus (buses have bike racks). Ample parking at Convention Center lots for a fee, plus city/county garages within blocks. Arrive early on race morning — 9,000+ riders means crowded access. Combine the race with a desert cycling vacation; Tucson is one of America's premier cycling cities with year-round riding on Mt. Lemmon, Gates Pass, and the Loop trail system.
logistics.accommodation: Hotels within 1 mile of Tucson Convention Center include Leo Kent Hotel (0.2 mi), Hampton Inn & Suites (0.3 mi), Home2 Suites (0.3 mi), and Ramada by Wyndham (0.4 mi), ranging $115-230/night. Airport-area hotels (Courtyard by Marriott, Four Points by Sheraton) run $99-199/night. JW Marriott Starr Pass Resort (6 mi) is the premium option. Book early for race weekend but options are plentiful — Tucson has abundant lodging at reasonable Southwest prices.
logistics.gear_recommendations: Road bike with standard gearing — no need for low climbing gears on this flat course. Aero positioning and smooth tires pay dividends on 102 miles of pavement. Arm warmers and a light vest for the 7 AM start (45-55°F mornings), shed layers by mid-morning. High-SPF sunscreen and UV-protective arm covers mandatory — desert UV is intense even in November. Two bottles minimum between aid stations; consider a third in the rear cage. Sunglasses essential for desert glare.

history.first_edition: 1983 — organized by cyclist Richard DeBernardis with 185-198 riders, raising $4,500 for charity
notable_editions: 1983: First edition with ~190 riders organized by Richard DeBernardis; $4,500 raised for charity
1986: Perimeter Bicycling Association of America formed; second event added to the calendar
2019: Perimeter events surpass $100 million in cumulative charitable fundraising
2022: 39th edition — 7,500 cyclists; 20-year-old Sean Christian outsprints Giro d'Italia champion Damiano Cunego for the overall win
2024: 41st edition — Tyler Stites sets men's course record (3:30:33); Olympic gold medalist Chloe Dygert smashes women's record (3:43:43, beating previous by 7 minutes); 9,000+ riders
2025: 42nd edition — Stephen Schaefer wins in 3:39:42; Marjorie Rinaldo wins women's race in 3:53:02; cumulative charity total approaches $130 million
history.records: Men's course record: Tyler Stites, 3:30:33 (2024, 102-mile course). Women's course record: Chloe Dygert, 3:43:43 (2024, 102-mile course). Previous 109-mile record: Phil Zajicek, 4:13:43 (2003). Notable professional participants include Damiano Cunego, Chloe Dygert, Quinn Simmons, Travis McCabe, Frankie Andreu, and Lance Armstrong.
history.growth: From ~190 riders and $4,500 in charitable contributions in 1983 to 9,000-11,000 annual participants and $130M+ in cumulative charity fundraising across 42 editions. The event grew steadily through the 1990s and 2000s, establishing itself as 'America's Biggest Bike Race.' Perimeter Bicycling Association now operates multiple events, with El Tour as the flagship. The event has survived organizational transitions, financial challenges (founder Richard DeBernardis stepped down amid financial issues), and course changes while maintaining its position as the premier November cycling event in the US.

course_description.overview: A 102-mile perimeter loop around greater Tucson through the Sonoran Desert. The course starts and finishes at the Tucson Convention Center in downtown, heading east through Davis-Monthan Air Force Base, south along Old Spanish Trail past Saguaro National Park (Rincon District) and historic Colossal Cave, through the communities of Corona de Tucson, Vail, Sahuarita, and Green Valley, before looping back north and west to downtown. Predominantly flat to gently rolling with 3,007 feet of total climbing. Four mountain ranges — Santa Catalina, Rincon, Tucson, and Santa Rita — frame the horizon throughout.

segments.name: Downtown Launch
segments.description: Mass start from Tucson Convention Center heading east through city streets. 9,000+ riders funnel onto the course — the most chaotic miles of the day. Finding your pace group takes patience. Streets are closed and police-controlled. Flat urban riding.

segments.name: Davis-Monthan & Old Spanish Trail
segments.description: The course passes Davis-Monthan Air Force Base and turns south onto Old Spanish Trail toward the Rincon Mountains. The desert opens up here — saguaro cacti appear in force, the Rincon range looms ahead. Gentle undulation begins as the route gains elevation toward Colossal Cave area. This is the scenic heart of the eastern leg.
segments.terrain: Gently rolling paved roads through Sonoran Desert

segments.name: The Southern Sweep — Vail to Sahuarita
segments.description: The long southern arc through Corona de Tucson, Vail, and Sahuarita. Open desert with views of the Santa Rita Mountains to the south. This is the most exposed section — wind can become a significant factor. The terrain is flat to gently rolling. Mental toughness territory: the landscape is beautiful but unchanging, and you're only halfway.
segments.terrain: Flat to gently rolling, exposed desert roads
segments.elevation_change: Rolling, net descent from ~3,800 ft to ~2,800 ft

segments.name: Green Valley Return
segments.description: Heading north through Green Valley and back toward Tucson. The Santa Rita Mountains are behind you now; the Tucson Mountains appear ahead. Tired legs on flat roads — this is where the last 20 miles of a century start to feel like the last 20 miles. Aid station stops become more tempting. Hold your pace.
segments.terrain: Flat paved roads, suburban and semi-rural
segments.elevation_change: Minimal, gentle net rise back to 2,500 ft

segments.name: The Finish — Back to Downtown
segments.description: Re-entering Tucson proper for the final push to the Convention Center. City streets, traffic management, and the growing energy of approaching the finish. Crowds thicken near downtown. The last miles are flat and emotionally charged — 102 miles of desert riding culminating in a community celebration at the finish line.
segments.terrain: Flat paved city and suburban streets

key_features: Sonoran Desert landscape with towering saguaro cacti throughout the eastern and southern legs
360-degree mountain panorama: Santa Catalina, Rincon, Tucson, and Santa Rita ranges
Old Spanish Trail corridor near Saguaro National Park (Rincon District)
Passage through historic Colossal Cave area at the eastern high point
Flat profile makes pacing and wind management the primary tactical challenges
Mass start with 9,000+ riders creates a unique peloton experience in the opening miles
course_description.strategy: Pacing is everything on a flat 102-mile course. Don't go out too fast in the crowded opening miles — find your target group and settle in. The first 35 miles are scenic and gently uphill; save energy for the exposed southern leg (miles 35-65) where wind can drain you. Hydrate proactively despite comfortable temperatures — the dry desert air dehydrates faster than you realize. Aid stations every 7-12 miles mean you can run lighter on bottles. The last 20 miles are a mental game on flat roads; drafting and group riding pay dividends here. If you're competitive (Platinum category), the race typically splinters on the southern section when crosswinds or headwinds hit the exposed roads.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Flat to gently rolling paved desert roads
race_challenges: 102-mile distance on flat terrain — pure endurance and pacing test
Potential wind on exposed southern desert sections (miles 35-65)
Sun exposure despite moderate temperatures — desert UV at 2,400 ft
Dry desert air requires proactive hydration between aid stations
Mass start with 9,000+ riders — crowded opening miles

non_negotiables.requirement: Century distance fitness — minimum 80-mile training ride at target pace
non_negotiables.why: 102 flat miles is a pure endurance test. The terrain won't slow you down, but the distance will. Complete at least one 80+ mile ride to confirm your fueling and pacing strategy works over the full duration.

non_negotiables.requirement: Wind strategy for exposed desert riding
non_negotiables.why: The southern section (miles 35-65) is exposed to crosswinds and potential headwinds. Practice riding in wind — learn to find wheels, manage effort in crosswind, and maintain position in a pace line without burning matches. Wind across open desert is relentless and can break your race.

non_negotiables.requirement: Hydration plan calibrated for dry desert conditions
non_negotiables.why: Despite comfortable 60-70°F temperatures, the dry desert air dehydrates you faster than you realize. You'll need consistent fluid intake between aid stations (every 7-12 miles). Don't be fooled by pleasant weather — the dryness is the hidden challenge. Test your hydration strategy on long training rides.
